introduction.heading: Introduction and Its Compliance
body: A Limited Liability Partnership is a hybrid business structure that combines the benefits of a company and a traditional partnership.
Introduced under the LLP Act, 2008, it offers the flexibility of a partnership with the added advantage of limited liability for its partners.

Compliance includes annual filing of Form 8 (Statement of Account & Solvency), Form 11 (Annual Return) and Income Tax Return. The structure supports efficient management and ensures the business is legally recognized and protected.

whyNeeded.heading: Why It Is Needed
body: An LLP is ideal for businesses that want to run with minimal liability while maintaining operational flexibility.
It is suitable for SMEs, startups, and professionals who wish to collaborate without full personal risk.

body.title: Limited Liability
body.description: Partners are not personally liable for business debts or liabilities. Their liability is limited to the capital they contribute, protecting their personal assets from business risks.

body.title: Separate Legal Entity
body.description: An LLP is a distinct legal entity from its partners. It can own assets, enter into contracts and sue or be sued in its own name.

body.title: Operational Flexibility
body.description: LLPs have fewer compliance requirements compared to private limited companies. There is no mandate for annual general meetings or heavy auditing unless turnover crosses specified limits.

body.title: No Minimum Capital Requirement
body.description: There is no mandatory minimum capital to start an LLP. Partners can contribute any amount agreed mutually.

body.title: Tax Benefits
body.description: LLPs enjoy several tax advantages, including no dividend distribution tax and exemption from audit if annual turnover is below ₹40 lakh and capital contribution is below ₹25 lakh.

body.title: Easy Ownership Transfer
body.description: Ownership can be partially transferred by inducting new partners or changing partnership ratios without significant procedural complications.

criteria: Minimum two partners (at least one must be a resident of India).
Partners can be individuals or legal entities (LLPs/Companies).
At least two designated partners, both of whom must have DIN (Director Identification Number) and DSC (Digital Signature Certificate).
Must have a registered office address in India.

documents: Address proof (bank statement, electricity bill, etc.)

body.step: Obtain DSC for all designated partners.
Apply for DIN through the MCA portal.
Name Reservation via RUN-LLP (Reserve Unique Name).
Filing Incorporation Documents in Form FiLLiP with MCA.
Draft and File LLP Agreement within 30 days of incorporation using Form 3.
PAN and TAN Application for the LLP.

body.question: Can an LLP be converted into a private limited company?
body.answer: Yes, by following the prescribed legal process and approvals.

body.question: Is it mandatory to audit LLP accounts?
body.answer: Only if turnover exceeds ₹40 lakh or contribution exceeds ₹25 lakh.

body.question: Can an LLP be formed with just one partner?
body.answer: No, a minimum of two partners is mandatory.

body.question: Is foreign investment allowed in LLPs?
body.answer: Yes, under the automatic route in permitted sectors.

body.question: Is the LLP name required to be unique?
body.answer: Yes, it must not resemble any existing LLP or company name.

body.question: Who can be a designated partner?
body.answer: Any individual with DIN and DSC; at least one must be a resident of India.

body.question: Is GST registration mandatory for LLP?
body.answer: Yes, if turnover exceeds ₹40 lakh (₹20 lakh for services) or in case of inter-state supply.

body.question: Can a salaried person become a partner in LLP?
body.answer: Yes, unless restricted by their employment contract.

body.question: Is there any cap on number of partners?
body.answer: No, there is no upper limit on the number of LLP partners.
